concerns.text: The plan assumes a 10-year dataset (~10 years of IceCube/Auger events) fits within a 7 GB RAM limit and completes in 6 hours on 2 CPUs. IceCube's 10-year muon track dataset is typically >100 TB (raw) or >10 TB (filtered). Even a pre-filtered subset for anisotropy analysis (e.g., high-energy muons) often exceeds 1 TB. Processing this volume on 2 CPU/7GB RAM without a distributed framework (Spark/Dask) or significant disk-swap (which would violate the 6h runtime) is infeasible. The plan must explicitly define a data sampling strategy (e.g., 'use only events >10 TeV' or 'random sample 1% of events') or a pre-aggregated dataset source to meet the compute constraints.

The research phase identifies a specific IceCube HuggingFace URL (`.../file-000.parquet`) but notes 'No verified source' for Pierre Auger. The plan (US-1, FR-001) mandates downloading *both* IceCube and Auger data to produce a combined analysis. If the Auger data source is not verified and accessible via a direct URL (like the IceCube one), the pipeline will fail the 'Multi-Detector Consistency' requirement (Constitution Principle VI) or the 'End-to-End' test. The plan must either secure a verified Auger URL or explicitly downgrade the scope to 'IceCube-only' analysis to avoid a fatal runtime failure.

The plan states 'Scale/Scope: ~10 years of data... ~115 intervals'. It does not cite the source for the '115 intervals' calculation (10 years / 27 days ≈ 134, not 115). While exact counts are deferred, the logic for the interval count should be referenced to the binning strategy (FR-010) or the specific time window (e.g., '2010-2020 inclusive' vs '2010-2019'). Clarify the date range to ensure the interval count is reproducible.
